title: The Sizing Decision
scenario: Account: $10,000 paper. A setup you like appears: entry 100, the structure says invalidation is at 95. You want in. The next three decisions decide whether this is a trade or a gamble.

checkpoints.question: How do you size the position?

options.label: Risk 1% of the account: ($10,000 x 1%) / (100 - 95) = 20 shares
options.feedback: Correct. Risk is chosen first; size is derived. The stop distance — not conviction — sets the share count.

options.label: Buy 100 shares — a nice round number that feels meaningful
options.feedback: Round-number sizing means your risk is whatever the chart decides later: here 100 shares risks 5% of the account, five times policy.

options.label: Use full buying power — the setup looks too good to waste
options.feedback: All-in sizing makes one adverse bar fatal. This is the exact habit that ends accounts.

checkpoints.question: Where does the stop go?

options.label: At 95, below the level that invalidates the thesis
options.feedback: Correct. The stop lives where the idea is proven wrong, not at a dollar amount that feels acceptable.

options.label: At 99 — tighter stop means I can buy more shares
options.feedback: A stop inside ordinary noise isn't risk control, it's a donation. You sized bigger to lose more often.

options.label: No stop — I'll watch it and exit manually if needed
options.feedback: An unwatched screen, a meeting, a gap: 'mental stops' fail precisely when they're needed. Invalidation undefined means risk unbounded.

checkpoints.question: Price drops to 96.20 then stalls. Your stop at 95 is intact. What now?

options.label: Nothing. The plan is intact until 95 trades
options.feedback: Correct. A pullback above your invalidation is noise by definition. The plan, not the discomfort, makes the decisions.

options.label: Move the stop to 93 to give it more room
options.feedback: Widening a stop converts a planned small loss into an unplanned large one. The constitution exists to forbid exactly this.

options.label: Exit now — protect what's left
options.feedback: Exiting above invalidation means your stomach overruled your plan. You kept the money but trained the habit of abandoning plans.

title: The Pre-Flight Check
scenario: You spot a pullback in an uptrend. The chart looks right. Before the order goes in, the process score is already being written.

checkpoints.question: What goes on the ticket before you confirm?

options.label: Stop, target, risk %, written reason, and how I feel right now
options.feedback: Correct. The five plan fields are the whole process score: invalidation, exit, size, thesis, state of mind.

options.label: Stop and target — the reason is obvious from the chart
options.feedback: A thesis that's 'obvious' today is unreadable in next week's review. Unwritten reasons score zero and teach nothing.

options.label: Just the entry — fill first, plan later, price won't wait
options.feedback: Plan-later is plan-never. Entering before defining risk is the single biggest process-score penalty.

checkpoints.question: Your written reason reads 'it looks bullish'. Good enough?

options.label: No — rewrite it: 'pullback to EMA20 in ADX-confirmed uptrend, volume contracting'
options.feedback: Correct. A thesis names the structure, the regime, and what would break it. 'Looks bullish' can't be reviewed or learned from.

options.label: It's fine — I know what I meant
options.feedback: You won't in 40 trades. The journal is a letter to a future self who has forgotten everything.

options.label: Reasons are for analysts — P&L is the only truth
options.feedback: Outcome without process is noise. The 2x2 exists because profitable bad decisions are the most expensive kind.

checkpoints.question: The trade wins +2R but you never set the stop you wrote down. The review calls it a dangerous success. Your response?

options.label: Log it honestly — the win doesn't excuse the broken process
options.feedback: Correct. Bad decision + good outcome trains the habit that eventually bills the account. Naming it is the fix.

options.label: Override it — a win is a win
options.feedback: Arguing with your own instrumentation defeats its purpose. The next 'win' like this will be sized bigger.

options.label: Skip the review entirely this week
options.feedback: Unreviewed trades teach nothing. The dangerous success you don't examine is the one you repeat.

title: The Stop Approaches
scenario: Long from 50, stop at 47.50, planned -1R = $100. Price is at 47.80 and falling. Every trader meets this moment; few meet it correctly.

checkpoints.question: Price ticks 47.55. The stop is about to trade. What do you do?

options.label: Nothing. The stop is the plan working, not the plan failing
options.feedback: Correct. You bought a defined risk. -1R was always one of the two planned outcomes.

options.label: Cancel the stop — it always bounces right after I'm out
options.feedback: Cancelling the stop converts $100 of planned risk into an open-ended hole. Every blow-up post-mortem contains this step.

options.label: Buy more here to lower the break-even
options.feedback: Averaging into a losing trade moves break-even closer and total risk further. This is how single trades kill accounts.

checkpoints.question: Stopped at 47.50, -1R. Five minutes later price reclaims 50. How do you read it?

options.label: A well-played loss — good decision, variance took it. Journal and move on
options.feedback: Correct. The classifier exists for this exact sting: outcome quality and decision quality are different axes.

options.label: Proof my stop was wrong — next time I'll use a wider one without resizing
options.feedback: Widening stops without re-deriving size just raises the dollar loss. If structure needs more room, size smaller.

options.label: Re-enter immediately at 50 — it owes me
options.feedback: An entry whose reason is your last loss is a revenge trade. The market keeps no accounts receivable.

checkpoints.question: Three planned losses in a row, all at stops, all journaled. Process average: 85. What changes?

options.label: Nothing structural — review for pattern, keep size, keep system
options.feedback: Correct. Three -1R losses are well inside ordinary variance for any real system. Changing systems after every losing streak guarantees you never have one.

options.label: Halve size until a winner shows up
options.feedback: Defensible but premature at three trades — and it asymmetrically shrinks the winners that pay for the losses.

options.label: The system is broken — find a new one tonight
options.feedback: Monte Carlo says a 50% win-rate system spends a third of its life in drawdown. System-hopping resets the sample every time.

title: The Red Morning
scenario: Two stop-outs before lunch, both planned, both -1R. You notice your jaw is tight and the chart refresh is getting compulsive. The next decision is the whole game.

checkpoints.question: A third setup appears. It's marginal — not your A-setup. What happens?

options.label: Skip it and log the emotion: 'frustrated, wanted it back'
options.feedback: Correct. Naming the state out loud is what catches tilt early. Marginal setup + hot state = the classic third loss.

options.label: Take it at half size — compromise with the urge
options.feedback: Half-size tilt is still tilt. The entry reason is your morning, not the market.

options.label: Take it at double size — win the morning back in one trade
options.feedback: Doubling after losses is martingale wearing a hoodie. This is the highest-severity pattern the risk officer knows.

checkpoints.question: You skipped it. The marginal setup would have won. How do you file that?

options.label: A good decision. Skipping B-setups while tilted is policy, and policy is judged over 100 trades
options.feedback: Correct. The counterfactual win changes nothing: the decision was right when made. Outcome-judging skipped trades reopens the tilt door.

options.label: Proof I should trust my gut when frustrated
options.feedback: One sample against policy is how dangerous habits get hired. The gut that wanted that trade also wanted the double-size revenge entry.

options.label: Turn the daily-cap rule off — it's costing money
options.feedback: Deleting the guardrail at the exact moment it engaged is the constitution failing its only test.

checkpoints.question: What ends the session correctly?

options.label: Journal all three decisions, tag the emotion, close the terminal
options.feedback: Correct. The day's value was never the P&L — it was a clean record of conduct under irritation.

options.label: Keep watching the tape 'just to learn' — no orders though
options.feedback: Screen-watching while tilted is how 'no orders' becomes one order. Learning happens in the journal, not the refresh loop.

options.label: Stay until a winner makes the day green
options.feedback: Needing a green day is the tilt talking. Days are accounting fictions; the process average is the real score.

title: The Honest Entry
scenario: Market's closed. Three executed trades sit unjournaled: a clean win, a sloppy win, and a planned loss. Ten minutes of honesty available.

checkpoints.question: Which trade gets journaled first and deepest?

options.label: The sloppy win — chased entry, no logged emotion, lucky exit
options.feedback: Correct. The sloppy win is the dangerous one: rewarded mistakes get repeated. Wins need autopsies more than losses do.

options.label: The loss — losses are where lessons live
options.feedback: Reasonable, but this loss was planned and clean — it confirms the system. The sloppy win is where the new information is.

options.label: The clean win — end on a high note
options.feedback: Journaling only what flatters you produces a diary, not a dataset.

checkpoints.question: What does a useful note on the sloppy win say?

options.label: 'Chased 0.8% above plan entry after it started moving. FOMO. Won anyway — tagged dangerous habit'
options.feedback: Correct. Specific behavior, named emotion, honest tag. This note feeds the coach's detectors and your future self.

options.label: 'Entry could have been better. Good result though'
options.feedback: Vague notes can't be aggregated, detected, or learned from. 'Could have been better' describes every trade ever taken.

options.label: 'Nailed it. +1.8R. More of these'
options.feedback: 'More of these' — more chased entries? The note just endorsed the mistake because it paid once.

checkpoints.question: It's been a long day and the third note feels optional. Decide.

options.label: Write it — two sentences. Unjournaled trades teach nothing
options.feedback: Correct. The streak of complete records is worth more than any single insight. Three of three, every day.

options.label: Do it tomorrow with fresh eyes
options.feedback: Tomorrow's memory is fiction garnished with hindsight. The entry-time state is already evaporating.

options.label: Skip it — it was just a planned loss, nothing to learn
options.feedback: Planned losses confirm the system works — that confirmation is data. Journal-skipping is a detector for a reason.

title: The Quiet Tape
scenario: Tuesday, 11:40. You've taken one good trade today. The tape has gone quiet and choppy. Your daily cap is 3. The cursor hovers.

checkpoints.question: Nothing matches your setup list, but you're 'in rhythm'. What now?

options.label: Stay flat. No setup, no trade — rhythm isn't a setup
options.feedback: Correct. The hardest position is flat. Selectivity IS the edge; activity is its tax.

options.label: Drop to the 1-minute chart to find something
options.feedback: Zooming in until noise looks like signal is how 'no setups' becomes five bad ones.

options.label: Take a small 'boredom trade' to stay engaged
options.feedback: Boredom trades are tilt's polite cousin: the entry reason is your mood. Small bad trades train big bad habits.

checkpoints.question: A genuinely good setup appears on a symbol you've never traded. Cap allows it. Decide.

options.label: Run the full pre-flight on the new symbol — liquidity, spread, volatility — then size normally if it passes
options.feedback: Correct. New symbols deserve the same process plus a venue check. Breadth done correctly is diversification of judgment.

options.label: Skip it — only trade what you know
options.feedback: Defensible, but 'what I know' shrinking to one chart narrows the evidence your skills rest on.

options.label: Take it instantly — setups are setups
options.feedback: The setup might transfer; the spread, liquidity, and gap behavior might not. Unexamined venue risk is still risk.

title: The 2x2 Reading
scenario: Weekly review. Four trades on the screen: an earned win, a well-played loss, an honest tuition, and a dangerous success. How you read them decides what next week looks like.

checkpoints.question: Which quadrant is the most dangerous?

options.label: The dangerous success — a bad decision the market paid
options.feedback: Correct. Rewarded mistakes compound: next time it's bigger size and more confidence, and the bill arrives with interest.

options.label: The honest tuition — it lost money AND was a bad decision
options.feedback: Tuition hurts but teaches cleanly: bad process, bad outcome, lesson legible. It rarely gets repeated at size.

options.label: The well-played loss — losses are always the problem
options.feedback: Calling well-played losses 'the problem' is outcome-worship. That loss is the system working.

checkpoints.question: Your earned win and dangerous success made the same +1.5R. What separates them?

options.label: The earned win's process can be repeated on purpose; the dangerous success was rented from variance
options.feedback: Correct. Identical P&L, opposite information. Only one of them is evidence of skill.

options.label: Nothing — R is R
options.feedback: R measures outcome, not decision quality. If R were everything, lottery winners would be investors.

options.label: Both were luck — single trades mean nothing at all
options.feedback: Single outcomes are noisy, but decisions are evaluable immediately. That asymmetry is the whole point of process scoring.

checkpoints.question: Next week's one corrective action?

options.label: Re-read the dangerous success's entry conditions and write the rule that would have blocked it
options.feedback: Correct. One specific, written rule per detected pattern — that's the feedback loop closing.

options.label: Target more wins — raise the win rate
options.feedback: Win rate is an output. Chasing it directly usually means cutting winners early and widening stops — both corrosive.

options.label: Trade more to grow the sample faster
options.feedback: A bigger sample of unexamined decisions is just more noise. Fix the generator before scaling the output.

title: Reading the Replay
scenario: You're about to run a historical episode in the simulator. How you use the replay decides whether it trains judgment or just entertains.

checkpoints.question: Mid-replay, you recognize the episode — you know how it ends. What's the honest move?

options.label: Answer from process anyway: what SHOULD a trader do at this bar without hindsight?
options.feedback: Correct. The replay grades decisions, not predictions. Hindsight-keying teaches you to be right about the past.

options.label: Use the knowledge — points are points
options.feedback: You just trained 'act on information you won't have live'. The score goes up; the skill goes down.

options.label: Quit and find an episode you don't know
options.feedback: Reasonable instinct, but recognizing episodes IS market literacy — the discipline is answering from process anyway.

checkpoints.question: You failed the debrief at 55%. Next action?

options.label: Read every checkpoint's consequence, study the linked lessons, retry tomorrow
options.feedback: Correct. The debrief names the better play and why. Spaced retry beats immediate re-guessing.

options.label: Retry immediately — you remember the right answers now
options.feedback: Immediate retries test memory, not judgment. Tomorrow's pass means something; tonight's means you have a working short-term memory.

options.label: Dismiss it — simulators aren't real trading anyway
options.feedback: The simulator is exactly as real as your decisions in it. Pilots who dismiss simulators don't get the plane.

title: How to Actually Learn
scenario: Twenty minutes for the Academy tonight. The goal is knowledge that survives contact with a live chart.

checkpoints.question: Best use of the twenty minutes?

options.label: One new lesson, then grade the due review cards, then its live exercise on real candles
options.feedback: Correct. Acquire, retrieve, apply — the triad that makes knowledge available under pressure.

options.label: Speed-read four lessons — volume is progress
options.feedback: Unretrieved reading evaporates in days. Four skimmed lessons are one forgotten one, four times.

options.label: Re-read your favorite lesson again — it feels solid
options.feedback: Re-reading feels like fluency and tests as nothing. The cards exist because recognition isn't recall.

checkpoints.question: A review card comes up: 'ATR'. You blank completely. What's the right grade?

options.label: 'Again' — honest blanks reschedule the card sooner, which is the system working
options.feedback: Correct. Grading honestly is the entire algorithm. A flattering 'good' just schedules your ignorance further away.

options.label: Reveal, recognize it, grade 'good' — close enough
options.feedback: Recognition after reveal is not recall before it. You just taught the scheduler to stop helping you.

options.label: Grade everything 'easy' to clear the queue faster
options.feedback: An empty queue of unlearned cards is a clean-looking failure. The queue is not the enemy; forgetting is.

title: The Streak Question
scenario: Day 13 of a daily streak. Tonight you're exhausted, and the streak counter is staring at you.

checkpoints.question: What's the minimum honest unit of showing up tonight?

options.label: Five review cards, graded honestly. Small and real beats big and skipped
options.feedback: Correct. Streaks survive on minimum viable sessions. The habit is the asset; tonight's volume is irrelevant.

options.label: Force a full lesson plus exercises — streaks demand real work
options.feedback: Heroic tired sessions produce shallow learning and resentment. Resentment kills streaks deader than rest does.

options.label: Open the app, click a lesson complete without reading — keep the counter alive
options.feedback: A gamed streak measures clicking, not learning. You'd be lying to the only audience that matters.

checkpoints.question: Day 14: the streak dies anyway — you simply forgot. Reaction?

options.label: Start streak day 1 tomorrow. The 13 days of learning are still yours
options.feedback: Correct. The counter resets; the knowledge doesn't. Treating a broken streak as catastrophe is how people quit entirely.

options.label: The system failed — abandon daily practice
options.feedback: One missed day deleting the habit is all-or-nothing thinking — the same cognitive bug that blows up accounts.

options.label: Look for a way to backdate yesterday's activity
options.feedback: Gaming your own instrumentation. If the numbers are negotiable, none of them mean anything.

title: Writing Rules That Hold
scenario: You're drafting your Trading Constitution. The rules you write tonight will block your own orders later — write them for the worst version of you.

checkpoints.question: Which max-risk rule belongs in the document?

options.label: 1% per trade — survivable losing streaks, boring by design
options.feedback: Correct. At 1%, ten straight losses cost ~10%. The constitution's job is making ruin arithmetic impossible.

options.label: 5% — you're here to grow, not crawl
options.feedback: At 5%, seven ordinary losses are a third of the account. The drawdown-recovery curve is merciless from there.

options.label: 'Flexible based on conviction' — rules should breathe
options.feedback: A rule with a conviction escape hatch is not a rule. Conviction is highest exactly when discipline matters most.

checkpoints.question: Week 3: a trade you're sure about needs 2% risk to fit its stop. The ticket blocks it. Decide.

options.label: Halve the size so the same stop costs 1% — the rule stands
options.feedback: Correct. The stop is structural; size is the free variable. The constitution just did its only job.

options.label: Amend the constitution right now to allow 2% — then take the trade
options.feedback: Amending mid-impulse to permit the impulse isn't governance, it's costume. Amendments happen on review days, never trade days.

options.label: Toggle enforcement off for one trade
options.feedback: An enforcement system with a snooze button protects nothing. This is the highest-severity governance failure.

checkpoints.question: When is the right time to amend the constitution?

options.label: On a scheduled monthly review, with the journal open and no positions on
options.feedback: Correct. Rules evolve on evidence, decided by the calm version of you — never by the one holding a position.

options.label: After a big winning week — you've earned looser rules
options.feedback: Loosening risk after wins is how dangerous successes graduate into account-sized lessons.

options.label: Never — a constitution is forever
options.feedback: Rules that never evolve with evidence become relics that get ignored wholesale. Scheduled amendment IS the discipline.

title: The Operator's Hour
scenario: One hour, real conditions: an open position near its stop, a fresh A-setup on another symbol, two unjournaled trades from yesterday, and review cards due. Allocate yourself.

checkpoints.question: First five minutes?

options.label: Check the open position's risk: stop live, size right, nothing to decide unless 94.80 trades
options.feedback: Correct. Open risk is always priority zero. Confirm the machine is armed, then leave it alone.

options.label: The A-setup — opportunities expire, admin doesn't
options.feedback: Entering new risk while open risk is unverified is how two 1% positions become one 4% surprise.

options.label: Refresh the P&L a few times to see where you stand
options.feedback: The P&L is an output. Watching it changes nothing and primes outcome-thinking before a decision hour.

checkpoints.question: The A-setup is valid and your daily cap allows it. The open position is 0.4R from its stop. Take the new trade?

options.label: Yes, at normal 1% size — each trade independent, both inside policy, combined heat checked
options.feedback: Correct. Two independent 1% risks inside a checked heat limit is the system working at capacity, not over it.

options.label: No — never two positions at once
options.feedback: Survivable but arbitrary: if heat limits and sizing are real, serial-only trading is leaving the system's capacity unused out of fear.

options.label: Yes, at 2% — the first one's basically lost anyway
options.feedback: 'Basically lost' is pre-spending a stop that hasn't traded, then doubling exposure on tilt math.

checkpoints.question: Twenty minutes left. Journal backlog or review cards?

options.label: Journal the two trades while context survives, cards with what's left
options.feedback: Correct. Journal context decays in hours; card scheduling tolerates a day. Perishable work first.

options.label: Cards first — the streak metric is visible and the journal isn't
options.feedback: Optimizing the visible metric over the valuable one. The journal feeds every detector the coach has.

options.label: Neither — scan for one more setup instead
options.feedback: The hour's leverage was never a third trade. Operators are paid for the boring twenty minutes.
